insights: 22 incidents were assigned to agents during documented leave periods, with 100% of those assignments going to on-leave agents despite available backup agents being present in every case.

Fred Luddy alone accounts for 12 of the 22 misassigned incidents (54.5%), all occurring during his 57-day leave period, and represents the sole Development department agent with documented leave — creating a single-point-of-failure structure.
Development department incidents have a 3.7× higher misassignment rate (52.17%) than Sales (14.08%) during leave periods, driven entirely by Fred Luddy’s unredistributed Development workload.

During Fred Luddy’s leave period, 100% of Development incidents (12/12) were misassigned to him while 0% were redirected, whereas Sales incidents during the same window had 0% misassignment (0/54), confirming department-level coverage asymmetry as the root cause.

No statistical differences exist between misassigned and properly redistributed incidents across category, priority, assignment group, or time-of-week — ruling out operational or incident-type explanations and reinforcing that structural coverage gaps, not incident characteristics, drive the imbalance.

The 22 misassigned incidents are evenly distributed across assignment groups (Database, Hardware, Inquiry/Help, Network, Software), confirming the issue is not group-specific but agent- and department-structural.
summary: The core goal of identifying imbalances in incident assignment during agent leave periods has been fully addressed: 22 incidents were misassigned to on-leave agents, with all 12 Development-related misassignments attributable to Fred Luddy — the only Development agent with documented leave and no overlapping coverage. This creates a structural single-point-of-failure, resulting in a 3.7× higher misassignment rate in Development versus Sales, where dual-agent coverage prevented misassignments entirely during the same timeframe. Incident attributes (category, priority, timing) show no distinguishing patterns, confirming the driver is organizational design, not operational randomness. Immediate focus should shift to redesigning Development’s coverage model — either by adding overlapping leave coverage, implementing automated assignment overrides during documented absences, or routing Development incidents to cross-trained Sales agents during Fred’s leave.
